actionable_predicate: Audit your team's engineering practices and classify each into two structural categories: (a) karma-yoga structure (value comes from the practice's formation, not a specific reward — chaos drills, code reviews at architectural-intent level, instrumentation work nobody asked for) and (b) fruit-bearing structure (value depends on completion of a specific outcome — feature launches with target dates, deploys with conversion targets, contractually-bound deliverables). The first category has the safety property the verse describes: partial application yields residual benefit. The second does not.
actionable_decision: Plan and protect the two categories differently. Fruit-bearing work needs project-management hygiene (timelines, dependency tracking, rollback plans). Karma-yoga work needs slot-protection in the calendar and freedom from completion-pressure — the chaos drill becomes worthless if it is being run to satisfy a manager's checkbox. Treat completion-pressure on karma-yoga work as a category error.
objection: Risks weaponization as 'don't track outcomes on this work' — but the verse's narrow claim is structural, not anti-tracking. Even chaos drills run for the practice's sake should be reviewed for quality (was the failure mode real? did the team learn?). The engineering layer's distinction is between value-from-completion and value-from-formation, not between tracked and untracked work.
